content:
I ignored UV for months, I Was Wrong. After switching from pip, I realized the real benefit had nothing to do with speed.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For years, I thought my Python workflow was perfectly fine. Create a virtual environment. Activate it. Install dependencies. Forget which environment was active. Accidentally install packages globally. Break something. Google error messages. Repeat.
Honestly, I considered this normal.
Python packaging has always felt a bit like assembling IKEA furniture without the manual. You eventually get the bookshelf standing, but you’re never entirely sure why there are three screws left over.
Then I tried uv.
At first, I dismissed it as another shiny developer tool that would dominate my social feeds for two weeks and then disappear. The Python ecosystem loves new tools almost as much as it loves creating new package managers.
But after using uv for several months across personal projects, APIs, and experimentation, I’ve realized something important:
uv isn’t just faster than pip. It encourages better engineering habits.
And that surprised me.
What Exactly Is uv?
If you haven’t heard of it yet, uv is an extremely fast Python package and project manager built in Rust by the team behind https://astral.sh/.
Official documentation: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/
The tool aims to replace or simplify many parts of a typical Python workflow, including:

• pip

• virtualenv

• pip-tools

• pyenv

• parts of Poetry

• dependency synchronization workflows
In practical terms, it helps you:

• create projects

• manage virtual environments

• install packages

• lock dependencies

• run scripts

• manage Python versions
All from a single tool. The first thing most developers notice is the speed.
Seriously.
The first time I ran:
uv pip install pandas numpy fastapiI thought something had failed because the installation completed almost instantly.
I reran the command just to make sure.
Nope.
It really was that fast.
Speed Is Nice
Fast installs are cool. Everyone likes speed. But after the novelty wore off, I realized the real benefit wasn’t performance.
It was discipline.
Before using uv, my workflow looked something like this:

• Create random folders.

• Forget to create a virtual environment.

• Install packages globally.

• Realize my mistake.

• Create a virtual environment.

• Reinstall everything.

• Promise myself I would “be more careful next time.”
Sound familiar?
Many Python developers — especially beginners — develop surprisingly messy habits because Python historically allowed it.

• Global installs.

• Untracked dependencies.

• Missing requirements files.

• Inconsistent environments.
Projects that only work on one machine.
I have built all of these.
More than once.
uv subtly pushes you toward cleaner project management.
And that’s a huge deal.
Respect Virtual Environments
I used to treat virtual environments like flossing.
I knew I should do it. I simply didn’t always do it. Because creating and managing environments felt like extra friction.
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txtNot difficult. Just annoying enough that I occasionally skipped it.
With uv:
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activateDone.
Even better, uv automatically recognizes and works with project environments. Over time, I noticed something interesting. I stopped creating “temporary” projects without isolation.
Every project became self-contained. That single habit alone eliminated countless dependency conflicts.
Dependency Management Became Predictable
One of my biggest frustrations with Python has always been dependency drift.
You install packages.
Weeks later:
pip freeze > requirements.txtSuddenly, your project depends on 137 packages you barely recognize. Half of them arrived indirectly. Some probably haven’t been updated since dinosaurs roamed the Earth.
With uv’s lockfile system, dependencies become much more explicit and reproducible.
The result?
Projects become easier to:

• reproduce

• deploy

• share

• debug

• onboard
When another developer clones the repository, they get the same environment. Not a “mostly similar” environment. The exact environment.
That reliability matters enormously in production systems.
Tooling Shapes Behavior
This was probably my biggest realization. Developers often assume tools are neutral.
They’re not.
Tools shape habits.
Good tools encourage good practices.
Bad tools encourage shortcuts.
For example:
If environment setup takes five minutes, developers skip it. If dependency locking is complicated, developers avoid it. If project initialization is cumbersome, developers improvise. Eventually those shortcuts become technical debt.
I’ve learned this lesson the hard way.
A few years ago, I maintained a side project that worked perfectly on my laptop. Deploying it to a server turned into a three-hour debugging session.
Why?
Because my local environment contained packages I had forgotten to document.
Classic “works on my machine” syndrome.
After migrating similar projects to uv workflows, these issues almost disappeared.
The workflow itself encourages reproducibility.
It Also Simplified New Projects
Starting a new Python project used to involve a surprising amount of ceremony.

• Create folder.

• Create environment.

• Activate environment.

• Install tooling.

• Generate requirements.

• Configure everything manually.
Now:
uv init my-project
cd my-project
uv add fastapiYou’re immediately productive. That reduction in setup friction sounds minor.
It isn’t. When experimentation becomes easier, you experiment more. I’ve started far more small projects because setup no longer feels like a chore. And experimentation is where many good ideas begin.
🤔 Is uv Perfect?
No.
No tool is.
There are still scenarios where existing workflows may make sense. Large organizations with established packaging standards may not migrate immediately.
Some teams rely heavily on Poetry-specific features. Others have deeply integrated CI pipelines.
Migration always carries costs. And if you’re maintaining legacy systems, changing tooling simply for hype may not be worthwhile.
But for new projects? I now default to uv almost automatically.
Why So Many Developers Are Excited
After reading community discussions and talking with other Python developers, I think the excitement comes down to three things:

• First, it dramatically improves developer experience.

• Second, it consolidates fragmented workflows.

• Third, it nudges developers toward better engineering practices.
Python has historically suffered from “too many ways to do the same thing.”
uv doesn’t solve everything. But it simplifies a lot. And simplicity scales.
My Favorite Side Effect
I spend far less time reading packaging tutorials. That’s probably the strongest endorsement I can give.
Because if you’ve spent enough years in Python, you know packaging documentation can sometimes feel like deciphering ancient scrolls.
Anything that reduces that pain deserves attention.
Final Thoughts
I originally adopted uv because everyone online was talking about speed. I kept using it because it improved the way I build software.
That’s a much stronger reason.

• Fast tools save seconds.

• Good workflows save hours.
And in my experience, uv delivers both. If you’re still relying exclusively on traditional pip workflows, I think uv is worth trying on your next project. You might discover, like I did, that the biggest improvement isn’t installation speed. It’s the habits you develop along the way.

👏 If you found this article useful, give it a few claps and share your experience in the comments.
Are you already using uv, or are you sticking with pip, Poetry, or another workflow? I’d love to hear what’s working for you.
